feedback.text: Hello. I am reaching out because I think that this Digital Fairness Act is the perfect opportunity to tackle a problem that millions of people have been facing AND continue to face today: the total, unwarranted, unwarned destruction of videogames. For the last couple of decades, videogames have been a bigger and bigger part of our society and culture. Billions of people play their cherished videogames weekly. This is to say, videogames are an undeniably important part of society... yet due to them being a relatively "new" area of entertainment, its industry is very vaguely regulated. To this day, any videogame company can launch a game at +90, generate millions of dollars, and permanently DESTROY the game for any and all players. Whenever and for whatever reason they want. I'm sure that anyone in their right mind could agree that this is completely preposterous and a violation of basic consumer rights. And that is not all. Giant videogame corporations' executives continue to shamelessly and openly push for and normalize increasingly more aggravating intentions. For one of many examples, I've included a printed article about an executive at Ubisoft, who blatantly told the audience to "get comfortable with not owning their games". Not to mention how even physical copies of games are practically extinct; they now require the user to download the actual game via internet. This was done to eradicate sharing the actual disk with friends... but it also means that all of your games are now directly in control of publishers and digital game stores. They can literally take it from your account whenever they feel like it. And this is not just a scary "potentially" or an unreal "if". This HAS happened multiple times already. Most notably, with Ubisoft's "The Crew". The game, which wasn't necessarily a live service game, was removed from the accounts of thousands of players without their consent. Ubisoft even directly demands players to physically destroy their game disks whenever one of their games shut down. I am sure you have heard about the "Stop Killing Games" movement. It aims to stop this brutal disregard of consumer rights or, at the BARE minimum, to stop consumers from losing access to what they have paid for. The videogames industry runs wild and no one is stopping it.
